---
title: "NATO Member Spending Analysis"
description: "A comprehensive fiscal and defence spending analysis of all 32 NATO allies — COFOG categories, federal budgets, country profiles, and a full glossary."
date: "2026-04-27"
---

# NATO Member Spending Analysis {.unnumbered}

This book provides a data-driven examination of government spending across all 32 NATO member states. It draws on the internationally standardised **Classification of the Functions of Government (COFOG)** framework, published annually by Eurostat, supplemented by IMF and OECD data for non-EU members. All expenditure figures are expressed as **percentage of GDP** using **general government** totals — central + sub-national + social security funds combined — the correct unit for cross-country comparison.

## Why This Matters

NATO's political debates have centred almost exclusively on the **2% of GDP defence target** since it was formalised at the 2014 Wales Summit. But defence is only one of ten COFOG spending dimensions, typically representing 1–4% of a government's total 37–58% of GDP footprint. Understanding the full spending profile of each ally — where the money actually goes — is essential for:

- Assessing the **real fiscal constraints** on defence expansion
- Understanding **structural differences** in welfare state models across the alliance
- Identifying **human capital gaps** (health, education) that directly affect military readiness
- Contextualising **deficit and debt trajectories** in the context of treaty commitments

## The New Fiscal Reality: The 5% Target

At the **2025 Hague Summit**, NATO allies committed to a landmark new target: **5% of GDP** in combined defence and security spending by 2035, with at least 3.5% going to core NATO-defined defence. For most European allies currently spending 40–58% of total GDP on government, adding 1.5–3.5 percentage points in defence without corresponding tax increases or cuts elsewhere is arithmetically non-trivial. This book maps exactly what fiscal space — or lack of it — each ally has.

## Book Structure

| Chapter | Content |
|---|---|
| [Chapter 1 — Alliance Defence Spending](chapter1.qmd) | NATO burden-sharing history, 2% and 5% targets, all 32 allies ranked by COFOG and NATO methodology |
| [Chapter 2 — Federal Budgets](chapter2.qmd) | Revenue, expenditure, fiscal balance, and gross debt for all 32 allies |
| [Chapter 3 — COFOG Category Analysis](chapter3.qmd) | Deep dive into all 10 spending functions with interactive dashboard |
| [Chapter 4 — Country Profiles](chapter4.qmd) | Individual analysis of all 32 NATO members |
| [Chapter 5 — Glossary](chapter5.qmd) | 90+ terms: NATO governance, COFOG, fiscal policy, economic indicators |

## Data Sources

All data is sourced from authoritative institutional sources:

- **Eurostat** `gov_10a_exp` — COFOG expenditure by function, 2023 (extracted March 2025)
- **IMF World Economic Outlook** — April 2025 (fiscal balances, revenue, debt)
- **OECD Government at a Glance 2025** — US and Canada COFOG data
- **NATO Defence Expenditure of NATO Countries 2014–2025** — August 2025 release
- **US Congressional Budget Office** — FY2024 Budget Review
- **European Stability Mechanism / ECB** — sovereign debt and interest data

> **Note on methodology:** All COFOG figures are *general government* (central + sub-national + social security funds), as % of GDP at current prices. COFOG defence figures differ from NATO's official defence expenditure methodology — see Chapter 1 and the Glossary for a full explanation of the gap.
